cataloging_subject.label: The Alliterative Morte Arthure
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The anonymous late-medieval Middle English alliterative poem represented by CME item AllitMA. The work is distinct from Robert Thornton's sole surviving copy, Valerie Krishna's 1976 critical edition, the Oxford Text Archive and University of Michigan digital texts, and this repository's XML artifact.

catalog_summary.title: The Alliterative Morte Arthure
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous
catalog_summary.date: Late fourteenth century, probably around 1400; sole surviving copy made c. 1430–1450
catalog_summary.region: Probably the North Midlands of England; sole surviving copy associated with Robert Thornton of East Newton, Yorkshire

notes: The initial article is conventional in modern cataloging; the manuscript rubric uses the shorter form Morte Arthure.

assertion.notes: Lincoln Cathedral's record transcribes the opening rubric as 'He begynnes Morte Arthure' and the closing as 'Here endes Morte Arthure writen by Robert of Thorntone.'

agents.name: Valerie Krishna
notes: Editor of the 1976 critical edition used by the OTA/U-M digital text; not the author of the medieval poem.

agents.name: Robert Thornton
notes: Scribe and compiler of the sole surviving manuscript witness, not the poem's author.
Historical attributions to Huchoun are not treated as established authorship in this record.

scope.description: The 1976 source edition underlying the OTA/U-M transcription
The sole surviving manuscript copy at Lincoln Cathedral

notes: This is witness-level scribal responsibility, not authorship of the work.

assertion.notes: The METS introduction explicitly describes a date around 1400 and North Midlands origin as informed guesses rather than certainties.

notes: This dates the extant copy, not necessarily the poem's composition.

places.label: North Midlands, England
notes: A broad proposed region of composition; this should not be conflated with the known association of the surviving scribe with Yorkshire.

assertion.notes: The source presents this localization as probable, not certain.

scope.description: Association of the sole surviving copy through its scribe and owner

notes: The manuscript was written by and for Robert Thornton of East Newton; this does not prove that the poem itself was composed there.

scope.description: Short embedded devotional or liturgical phrases, including 'In manus' near the close

form_statements.description: Unrhymed alliterative long-line verse; each line is divided rhythmically into two half-lines joined by alliteration.

assertion.notes: The poem treats Arthur's final wars through the sober chronicle-history tradition while retaining romance form and matter.

subject_statements.label: The fall and death of King Arthur

summaries.value: An anonymous Middle English alliterative poem recounting Arthur's refusal of Roman tribute, his continental war against Emperor Lucius, and the catastrophic return to Britain after Mordred's betrayal. Its chronicle-like interest in campaigns, cities, kingship, and bodily warfare culminates in the destruction of the Round Table and Arthur's death.

The poem survives in one known medieval copy, Lincoln Cathedral MS 91, fols. 53r–98v. Robert Thornton copied that witness in the fifteenth century; his scribal identity must not be converted into authorial attribution.

extent.description: The local XML contains one complete poem of 4,346 numbered verse lines, grouped in 232 LG elements and divided into 80 editorial/structural DIV1 ranges. It also records 117 printed-page breaks, 92 manuscript-folio milestones from 53r through 98v, and 40 SUPPLIED elements. The 80 divisions are encoding units rather than authorial chapters.

content_parts.label: The Alliterative Morte Arthure
content_parts.summary: The complete alliterative narrative from the opening invocation and promise to tell of Arthur's deeds through the Roman campaign, Mordred's usurpation, Arthur's final battle, death, and burial.
notes: This summary-level part deliberately does not treat the 80 XML divisions as intrinsic chapters.

related_works.title: Historia regum Britanniae
notes: The poem belongs to a chronicle-history tradition that ultimately descends from Geoffrey of Monmouth; this is not a claim that the extant poem directly copied a particular manuscript of Geoffrey's work.

related_works.title: The Tale of Arthur and the Emperor Lucius
notes: The corresponding tale in Malory's Morte Darthur closely adapts the first half of the Alliterative Morte Arthure.

evidence.citation: General Corpus, CME/source/CME_from_OTA/AllitMA.xml, FILEDESC and ENCODINGDESC.
evidence.summary: The XML identifies the anonymous work, the 1993 U-M/OTA digital text, Krishna's 1976 critical edition as its printed source, Lincoln Cathedral MS 91 as the manuscript, and the 1975 Thornton facsimile as an editorial reference.

evidence.citation: Element and attribute count of the checked-in AllitMA XML, performed 2026-07-11.
evidence.summary: Inspection found 4,346 L, 232 LG, 80 DIV1, 117 PB, 92 MILESTONE, and 40 SUPPLIED elements; line numbering runs 1–4346 and manuscript milestones run from fol. 53r to fol. 98v.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library Digital Collections, The alliterative Morte Arthure, item information.
evidence.summary: The item page identifies the anonymous work, Burt Franklin c. 1976 publication, OTA number U-1675-A, and a complete sequence through line 4346.

evidence.citation: Oxford Text Archive, Alliterative Morte Arthure, ota:1675.
evidence.locator: Item record, file preview, and rights label
evidence.summary: The OTA record supplies the digital text, identifies it as transcribed from Valerie Krishna's edition, and currently labels the files publicly available under CC BY 3.0.

evidence.citation: Lincoln Cathedral Library, MS 91, The 'Thornton' Romances.
evidence.locator: Record MS 91, item 4 and manuscript description
evidence.summary: The holding record places Morte Arthure at fols. 53–98v, quotes its rubric and explicit, attributes the copying to Robert Thornton, dates the manuscript c. 1430–1450, and describes it as written by and for Thornton of East Newton, Yorkshire.

evidence.citation: Larry D. Benson and Edward E. Foster, eds., King Arthur's Death: The Middle English Stanzaic Morte Arthur and Alliterative Morte Arthure, Middle English Texts Series.
evidence.summary: The scholarly edition classifies the work as Middle English poetry, Arthuriana, romance, and alliterative writing, and summarizes its chronicle-history treatment of Arthur's continental war and fall.

evidence.citation: Larry D. Benson, Introduction to King Arthur's Death, revised by Edward E. Foster, Middle English Texts Series.
evidence.locator: Introduction, especially discussion of date, region, chronicle tradition, meter, and Malory
evidence.summary: The introduction cautiously proposes a North Midlands origin and date around 1400, explains the poem's unrhymed alliterative long lines and chronicle-history tradition, and describes Malory's adaptation.

evidence.citation: Database of Middle English Romance, Alliterative Morte Arthure.
evidence.summary: The scholarly database records the sole witness as Lincoln Cathedral MS 91, fols. 53r–98v, and lists Krishna's 1976 edition among the modern editions.

evidence.citation: Renée Ward, The Alliterative Morte Arthure—Selections, in Primary Sources on Monsters, Cambridge University Press, 2021.
evidence.summary: Ward describes the 4,346-line poem, its sole witness, late-fourteenth-century composition, and sustained concern with imperial conflict, crusading rhetoric, warfare, and bodily injury.

evidence.citation: Google Books bibliographic record for Valerie Krishna, ed., The Alliterative Morte Arthure: A Critical Edition, 1976.
evidence.summary: The record identifies Valerie Krishna as editor, Burt Franklin as publisher, 1976 as publication year, and 361 pages.

open_questions.question: Can the poem's composition be localized or dated more narrowly without converting disputed scholarship into a fact?
next_steps: Review recent linguistic and historical scholarship beyond the general METS dating summary.
Record competing date and dialect proposals as separate assertions if they materially differ.

open_questions.question: Is any proposed named authorship supported strongly enough to record beyond an open attribution history?
next_steps: Consult current authorship scholarship before adding Huchoun or another attributed author.

open_questions.question: Is a complete, openly accessible digital facsimile of Lincoln Cathedral MS 91 now available?
next_steps: Ask Lincoln Cathedral Library whether a complete digital surrogate exists and what reuse terms apply.
Use the 1975 Scolar Press facsimile through a holding library for visual comparison in the interim.

notes: The U-M interface's publication date c. 1976 is the date of Krishna's source edition, not the date of the medieval work.
The OTA record's date 1440 describes the witness-era text and should not be treated as a securely known composition year.
Rights and access for the 1976 edition, digital encodings, manuscript text, and manuscript photographs are documented separately in the lineage manifest.
